content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


September 28, 2001


Laura McKinney
Deputy City Attorney
City of Berkeley
Office of the City Attorney
2180 Milvia Street
Berkeley, CA 94704

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-145

Dear Ms. McKinney:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTION

Are members of a resident council considered "public officials" under the Act?

CONCLUSION

Yes. Resident council members are public officials subject to the Act's conflict of interest and disclosure rules.

FACTS

The Berkeley Housing Authority (BHA) administers and oversees the U.S. Housing and Urban Development (HUD) program. You state that California courts have held that local housing authorities are considered state agencies because they are acting under state law. However, the designated employees of the BHA and the tenant members of the BHA governing body are included in the conflict of interest code for the

1 Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-01-145
Page No. 2

City of Berkeley.² Two major programs that the BHA administers are the public housing
and Section 8 programs. Through the public housing program, the BHA provides
housing to low-income individuals. Through the Section 8 program, the BHA provides
housing vouchers to low-income individuals who can then apply these vouchers to rent
with a private landlord.

HUD recently mandated that all housing authorities complete a 5-year plan to be
submitted to HUD for review and approval and, each year thereafter, to complete an
annual plan. This mandate was accomplished through regulations. As part of these
regulations, HUD required input from residents of both the public housing and Section 8
programs. It required that housing authorities appoint a Resident Advisory Board (RAB)
to assist and make recommendations with the development of the plans. HUD further
required that where jurisdiction-wide groups called "resident councils" already existed,
these individuals were to be appointed as the RAB. However, where no resident council
existed, the regulations stated the housing authority "shall provide reasonable notice to
such residents and urge that they form resident councils." (24 C.F.R. § 903.13(b)(3).)

No resident councils had been formed in Berkeley and, as a result of this
regulation, the BHA set out to encourage the development of resident councils.
Consequently, two resident councils were formed – one of public housing tenants and one
of Section 8 tenants. HUD has promulgated detailed regulations regarding eligibility for
membership, election processes, relationship to the housing authority and role of resident
councils. These regulations only relate to public housing tenant resident councils as these
individuals were the only individuals HUD anticipated would form a resident council.
The BHA has gone beyond the requirements and formed both a public housing and
Section 8 Resident Council.

Among their responsibilities, the resident councils may negotiate with the BHA
on all uses of community space for meetings, recreation and social services and other
resident participation activities pursuant to HUD guidelines. (24 C.F.R § 964.18(a)(6).)
Additionally, resident councils are to collaborate with the BHA as it assures its lead role
for assuring maximum opportunities for skills training for public housing residents. (24
C.F.R § 964.18(a)(11).)

The Resident Councils were formed as a result of the BHA giving notice to all
public housing and Section 8 tenants regarding an upcoming election and the
requirements for eligibility to participate as a member of the Resident Council. After
receiving applications for membership the BHA and the Berkeley City Clerk's Office
facilitated an election which resulted in 5 public housing tenants being elected to their
Resident Council and 9 Section 8 tenants being elected to their Resident Council. The
BHA has determined that these resident councils are subject to provisions of the Brown
Act.

² The remaining positions on the BHA governing body are held by city council members who
have a higher level of disclosure and who are included in a conflict of interest code for which the
Commission is the filing officer.

File No. A-01-145
Page No. 3

The BHA is now in the process of providing these individuals with training. As
part of the training offered, the BHA wished to discuss the applicable rules regarding
conflicts of interest. HUD has promulgated a specific regulation regarding conflicts of
interest for members of resident councils. (See 24 CFR § 964.145.) However, you
inquire as to whether these individuals would be considered "public officials" under the
Political Reform Act as well.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making, participating in making or
otherwise using their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they
have a material interest. Section 82048, with exceptions not pertinent here, defines
"public official" to include "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or
local government agency."

The Act defines "local government agency" as a county, city or district of any
kind including school district, or any other local or regional political subdivision, or any
department, division, bureau, office, board, commission or other agency of the foregoing.
(§ 82041, emphasis added.) The employees and members of the BHA, with the
exception of the city council members, are included in the conflict of interest code for the
City of Berkeley. Therefore, for purposes of this letter, we treat the BHA as a local
government agency for purposes of the Act. The sole question we address is whether the
resident councils are public officials under the Act.³

First, we address whether the resident councils are local government entities. In
1977, the Commission considered the definition of "local government agency" in In re
Siegel (3 FPPC Ops. 62, copy enclosed). The Siegel Opinion involved a nonprofit water
development corporation formed by the City of Pico Rivera. The Commission applied
the following four-part test:

1. Whether the impetus for formation of the entity originated with a
government agency.

2. Whether the entity is substantially funded by, or its primary source of
funds is, a government agency.

3. Whether one of the principal purposes for which it is formed is to
provide services or undertake obligations which public agencies are
legally authorized to perform and which, in fact, they traditionally have
performed; and

³ You have provided information indicating that California courts have previously considered local
housing authorities to be state agencies. We point out a more recent decision, Lynch v. San Francisco
Housing Authority (1997) 55 Cal.App.4th 327, in which the court affirmed a conclusion of Torres v. Board
of Commissioners (1979) 89 Cal.App.3d 545, that "a housing authority may be a state agency for some
purposes but not for others." If you seek advice regarding BHA's status as a state or local government
agency, as opposed to the resident councils, you should seek further advice.

File No. A-01-145
Page No. 4

4. Whether the entity is treated as a public entity by other statutory
provisions.

In Siegel, the Commission concluded that the corporation was a local government
agency and the members of the corporation's board of directors were subject to the
disclosure and disqualification requirements of the Act. While the Siegel factors were not
intended to be a definitive litmus test for determining whether an entity is public for
purposes of the Act, by applying the factors to the set of facts presented in your letter we
can draw some conclusions with respect to the status of the resident councils. Ultimately,
however, the test must still be a factual analysis on a case-by-case basis. (In re Vonk
(1981) 6 FPPC Ops. 1, copy enclosed .)

The first factor is whether the impetus for formation of the resident councils
originated with a governmental agency. Generally, the first factor has been met where an
entity is created by statute or ordinance, or by some official action of another local
government agency. For example, in the Siegel opinion, although the agency was created
as a nonprofit corporation, the city council was intimately involved in the creation of the
corporation in question. Conversely, In re Leach (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 48, the
Commission found that since the local chamber of commerce and business association
preexisted the contractual relationship with the city, it was clear that the city was not the
impetus for formation of the association and the chamber.

First, the impetus for formation of the resident councils originated with a
government agency. In general, an entity meets the first factor when it is created by a
statute or ordinance. Resident councils will be created under a federal law, rather
than a state or local law. However, the federal law is not self-executing. To receive
funding, each local housing authority must establish the governing structure that will
carry out the federal law. Since the BHA will be integrally involved in the formation of
the resident councils, these entities meet the first factor.

Second, resident councils will receive all of their funding from federal grants. We
have previously advised that if an entity receives most of its funding from federal monies,
it meets the second factor. (Prestidge Advice Letter, No. A-95-323.) Therefore, resident
councils meet the second factor.

Third, the purpose of a resident council is to improve the quality of life and
resident satisfaction, and participate in self-help initiatives to enable residents to create a
positive living environment for families living in public housing. The Commission has
previously advised that the purpose of providing adequate law and moderate-income
housing is a legal and traditional obligation of public agencies. (Kirkpatrick Advice
Letter, No. I-91-441; Albuquerque Advice Letter, No. A-88-422 .) These are obligations
that public agencies have traditionally performed. As such, resident councils meet the
third factor.

Fourth, the HUD regulations treat resident councils as government agencies.
Pursuant to this federal law, the housing authority appoints the members to the resident

File No. A-01-145
Page No. 5

councils. Resident councils as part of the RAB must work with the housing authority to
assist and make recommendations regarding the development of the housing authority
plan and any significant amendment or modification to the housing authority plan. As
such, resident councils carry out their statutory functions in partnership with, or subject to
approval by, the local housing authority. Additionally, the resident councils are public
entities subject to the Brown Act. Thus, resident councils meet the fourth Siegel factor.

Accordingly, the resident councils meet all of the criteria of a local governmental
agency under the Siegel test. After determining that the resident councils are local
government entities, the next step is to assess whether the members of the resident
councils are "members" as defined in Regulation 18701. If so, they will be considered
public officials under the conflict of interest rules of the Act. Regulation 18701 provides
that:

"(1) 'Member' shall include, but not be limited to, salaried
or unsalaried members of committees, board or
commissions with decisionmaking authority. A committee,
board or commission possesses decisionmaking authority
whenever:
"(A) It may make a final governmental decision;
"(B) It may compel a governmental decision; or it may
prevent a governmental decision either by reason of an
exclusive power to initiate the decision or by reason of a
veto that may not be overridden; or
"(C) It makes substantive recommendations that are and
over an extended period of time have been, regularly
approved without significant amendment or modification
by another public official or governmental agency."
(Regulation 18701(a)(1).)

Your facts have described some of the duties that the resident councils will carry
out. In particular, the resident councils have the purpose of making substantive
recommendations regarding uses of community space or opportunities for resident skills
training. Provided these recommendations are regularly approved without significant
amendment or modification by the BHA, the members of the resident councils are
"public officials" of a local government agency and are subject to the Act's conflict of
interest rules. Additionally, the members will also be subject to the conflict of interest
disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act.

File No. A-01-145
Page No. 6

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: [signature]
Natalie Bocanegra
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

Yes. Resident council members are public officials subject to the Act's conflict
of interest and disclosure rules.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are members of a resident council considered "public offrcials" under the Act?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Resident council members are public officials subject to the Act's conflict
of interest and disclosure rules.
